sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Fractures and dislocations need focused prehospital care to reduce pain, protect neurovascular status, limit further tissue injury, and support safe transport. Immobilisation should help the patient, not create unnecessary scene delay or repeated manipulation.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content:
- Check pain, colour, warmth, sensation, movement, and distal perfusion before and after any manipulation or splinting
- Compare findings with the unaffected limb where possible
- Worsening neurovascular findings, open fracture, or compartment concerns should raise urgency

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
1. Expose the injury enough to assess deformity, wounds, and distal function

2. Provide analgesia early when feasible and clinically appropriate

3. Cover open fractures and control bleeding without pushing exposed bone back into the wound

4. Splint the injury in the position found unless reduction or gentle realignment is clearly indicated by protocol, neurovascular compromise, or inability to transport safely

5. Immobilise the injured segment and the joints above and below where appropriate

6. Reassess neurovascular status after splinting and during transport

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Long bone and pelvic injuries can hide major blood loss even when external bleeding looks limited
- Repeated manipulation should be avoided; if a manoeuvre is needed, make it deliberate and clinically justified
- Immobilise dislocations in the best tolerated position unless reduction is clearly indicated within scope
- Document neurovascular findings before and after splinting and the device used
